briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. We will now begin the climate briefing for March 3, 2026.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Accelerating Domestic Climate Education and Policy Adaptation Efforts
sections.content: Today, domestic efforts to raise awareness and respond to climate change have been actively reported. In Cheongju, Chungcheongbuk-do, a companion plant hospital has begun operations, and the Climate Change Experience Center has reopened after remodeling, welcoming citizens [1, 3, 4, 5, 8, 14]. Notably, this experience center has been transformed into an interactive educational space for children, expected to contribute to raising climate change awareness among future generations [5]. Meanwhile, Assemblyman Yoon Jun-byung has proposed the 'Arbor Day Adjustment Act' to respond to climate change, demonstrating ongoing climate adaptation efforts at the policy level [6, 7, 10, 12].

sections.title: 🌱 Nationwide Spread of Carbon Neutrality Practices
sections.content: Concrete actions towards achieving carbon neutrality have been observed across various domestic institutions and local governments. The Korea Industrial Complex Corporation is holding nationwide briefings for a low-interest loan program to support carbon-neutral and eco-friendly facilities, totaling 80 billion KRW, encouraging companies' carbon reduction efforts [21, 22, 23, 28, 36, 44]. Gyeonggi Province is recruiting export-oriented small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) to participate in a one-stop support package for carbon neutrality, aiding the transition of the industrial sector [17, 18, 19, 33, 35, 40, 43, 60]. Furthermore, various resident-participatory practice models are spreading nationwide, including the integration of carbon neutrality into urban regeneration in Gwangmyeong City, carbon neutrality campaigns in Gimcheon City, the completion of a hydrogen charging station in Danyang County, and the operation of carbon neutrality point systems in Yongin City and Gwangmyeong City [24, 29, 30, 39, 41, 42, 45, 46, 47, 48, 51, 52, 55, 56, 57, 59].

sections.title: 🌱⚠️ Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition and Policy Challenges
sections.content: Efforts to transition to renewable energy are ongoing both domestically and internationally. Domestically, an amendment to the Renewable Energy Act has passed the National Assembly [61, 74]. Gangneung City has been selected for the New and Renewable Energy Convergence Support Project for the fourth consecutive year and plans to invest 3 billion KRW to supply new and renewable energy to 276 locations [64, 65, 67, 70, 71, 73, 78]. Internationally, the United States has recorded its highest-ever renewable energy generation, and Brazil is expanding its use of renewable energy by lowering import tariffs on Bitcoin mining equipment [69, 72]. However, a climate think tank has offered a critical perspective, stating that the amended Renewable Energy Act fails to address the issue of over-reliance on specific power sources and is insufficient as an alternative to the Renewable Portfolio Standard (RPS), suggesting the need for policy supplementation [62, 68].

sections.title: 🌍 Political Challenges and Controversies in International Climate Change Response
sections.content: In the international community, political conflicts and controversies surrounding climate change response continue. In the United States, the conflict between academia and political circles has intensified over the removal of the 'climate change section' from a legal manual [2]. Concerns have also been raised that such climate change denial movements could dampen demand for ethanol and Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) [86]. In Australia, political sparring was reported, with the Climate Change and Energy Minister accusing the opposition leader of concealing energy price increases [90]. These reports demonstrate that responding to climate change goes beyond mere scientific or technical issues, intertwining with the complex political dynamics of each nation.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Threats and Global Vulnerability
sections.content: Globally, climate threats are intensifying, and vulnerability to them is growing. A warning has been issued that the El Niño phenomenon could reappear by the end of 2026, putting the Earth at risk of record-breaking heatwaves [13]. Domestically, the Unmun Dam has entered a drought 'alert' phase, making water scarcity a reality [15]. Internationally, Australia experienced its wettest summer in nearly a decade, yet also its eighth hottest on record, showing extreme climate variability [97]. Furthermore, global research indicates that children are experiencing sleep deprivation, panic attacks, and fear of death due to climate change, and the global food system also faces climate adaptation challenges, underscoring the urgent need for global preparedness [88, 92, 96].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted various domestic and international efforts to address climate change, while also shedding light on the serious challenges and threats we continue to face. Continued attention and cooperation are more crucial than ever. This concludes today's climate briefing.
